segments.s294-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. شد is the past verb 'came to be/happened' used with the following phrase to mean 'came to an end.' کار پیران ویسه is a genitive grouping 'the affair/work (کار) of the elders (پیران) of Viseh (ویسه)'; به سر is an idiomatic complement ('to the head'/‘to an end’) that completes شد and yields 'came to an end.'
segments.s294-g2: This is a verbless main clause in which the prepositional phrase به جنگ دگر ('to/for another battle') gives direction/purpose and شاه پیروزگر is the nominative noun phrase 'the victorious king' (شاه modified by the adjective/epithet پیروزگر). The missing action is supplied from context: 'the king (went) to another war.'
segments.s294-g3: بیاراست is a past/adjectival form from آراستن meaning 'arrayed' or 'well‑adorned' and here functions predicatively; از هر سوی is a prepositional phrase 'from every side/direction.' مهتران is the plural noun 'the chiefs' (subject). The adjective‑like verb placed before the noun is a poetic inversion meaning 'the chiefs, arrayed on every side...'.
segments.s294-g4: برفتند is the simple past 'they went/advanced' (رفتند with the prefix بر‑ giving a sense of setting out). با introduces the accompaniment 'with,' لشکری is the object 'an army/troops' and بی‌کران is an adjective 'boundless/endless' modifying لشکری. Together: 'they advanced with an endless army.'
segments.s294-g5: برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth.' خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'roaring' (the act of roaring), and کرنای is the genitive 'of the trumpet' (کرنا + possessive ending). Word order is V + verbal noun + genitive: 'the roaring of the trumpet arose.'
segments.s294-g6: به هامون is a directional prepositional phrase 'to/toward the plain.' کشیدند is the past transitive 'they drew/pulled' (here used of drawing curtains or spreading a pavilion), and پرده‌سرای is the direct object — literally 'parde‑sara' (theatre/stage or pavilion/curtain area). The clause means they drew the pavilion/curtain onto the plain.
segments.s294-g7: به شهر اندرون is a locative phrase 'inside the city' (شهر + اندرون 'inner/within'). جای خفتن is a noun phrase 'place for sleeping' (جای + verbal noun خفتن), and نماند is the negative past 'there remained not' (i.e. 'there was none left'). The clause thus says there was no place left to sleep inside the city.
segments.s294-g8: This clause repeats the pattern for a different locale: به دشت اندرون 'within the plain,' راه رفتن is a noun phrase 'walking/room to walk' (راه + verbal noun رفتن), and نماند again means 'remained not.' The sense is 'there was no room left to walk on the plain.'
segments.s294-g137: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking origin; the phrase lists two places/epithets: بغداد (Baghdad) and گردن فرازان کرخ, where گردن فرازان ('lofty-necked, exalted') is an adjectival epithet attached to کرخ (Karkh). Word order is place1 و place2(epithet + name).
segments.s294-g138: بفرمود is an honorific/past third-person verb 'he commanded/said'; تا introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that/in order that') which continues the action; با marks instrument 'with'; کمانهای is the plural of کمان 'bows' and چرخ is a poetic epithet 'of the sky/heaven', i.e. 'heavenly bows'.
segments.s294-g139: به پیش اندرون is a directional phrase: به + پیش = 'toward/in front of' and اندرون = 'the inner quarters/inside'; تیرباران is a deverbal noun/compound 'arrow-rain' (a hail of arrows); کنند is third-person plural present/optative 'they do/make' used here to describe the action (classical Persian uses کنند for repeated/habitual or subjunctive sense).
segments.s294-g140: هوا را uses را as the definite object marker ('the air' as direct object). چو is the comparative/simile 'like/as'; ابر بهاران literally 'spring clouds' (ابر = cloud, بهاران = of spring); کنند repeats the verb 'they make' — together: 'they make the air like spring clouds.'
segments.s294-g141: به دست here means 'by/into the power/hand of' (به with sense 'by' + دست 'hand' used idiomatically as 'power, control'); فریبرز is the proper name; نستوه is an epithet attached to the name (an older adjective; here given as an epithet); بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s294-g142: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause 'for/that'; نزدیک او = 'near him' (نزدیک = near); لشکر انبوه بود = 'a multitude/dense army was' (انبوه = numerous/dense); بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s294-g143: A nominal string: بزرگان 'the great/important ones' followed by the compound modifier رزم آزموده (رزم 'battle' + آزموده 'tried/experienced') and then سران 'leaders/heads' — together functioning as an epithet: 'the great, battle-experienced leaders.'
segments.s294-g144: ز دشت is 'from the plain' (ز = from, دشت = plain). The following nouns list troop-types: سواران 'horsemen/riders' and نیزه‌وران (written as two words here: نیزه + وران) 'spear-wielders.' Note: وران as given in the lemma list does not match the common suffixal/agent sense here, so it is treated separately below.
segments.s294-g113: A nominal phrase giving the complement that specifies the guard attached to each elephant. نگهبان + هر پیل = “the guard of each elephant”; سیصد سوار is a numeral phrase in apposition describing what that guard consisted of (three hundred horsemen). There is no verb here; the whole line is a verbless descriptive clause common in epic diction.
segments.s294-g114: Two parallel noun phrases joined by و that describe those horsemen: همه جنگ‌جوی (all were warriors) و همه نیزه‌دار (and all were spearmen). The repeated همه adds distributive/emphatic force. This is a simple coordination expanding on the composition of the three hundred riders.
segments.s294-g115: ز + place (بغداد) marks origin: ز بغداد گردان جنگاوران = “a troop of warriors from Baghdad.” گردان here is a collective noun ‘troop/unit’ followed by the plural noun جنگاوران, a genitival/attributive grouping introduced by the preposition ز (poetic form of از ‘from’).
segments.s294-g116: A relative clause introduced by که + بود(ند): که بودند = “who were.” با indicates accompaniment or alliance: با زنگه شاوران = “with Zangeh–Shavaran.” Here زنگه شاوران behaves like a proper name/group name, so the individual words act as a name rather than their literal lexical senses.
segments.s294-g117: A noun phrase with گزیده (selected/elite) modifying سپاهی (soldiers/force): سپاهی گزیده ز گردان بلخ = “a chosen force from the Balkh regiment.” The preposition ز marks origin (‘from’), and بلخ names the unit’s place of origin.
segments.s294-g118: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning he ordered/commanded; تا introduces the subordinate/purpose clause (تا + verb) meaning “so that/that …” or “to …”. با introduces the instrument/accompaniment with کمانهای چرخ (“with the circular/round bows”); چرخ here qualifies کمانها and is best read as ‘wheel/circular’ describing the type/style of bow. The main verb of the subordinate clause is elided (typical in poetry) and supplied from the prior verb بفرمود.
segments.s294-g119: A verbless/predicative clause using an older past form ببودند (they were) with پیاده as predicate: پیاده ببودند = “they were foot‑soldiers/infantry.” بر پیش پیل = “in front of the elephant” (بر + پیش together mark position before). This is descriptive placement of the infantry relative to the elephant.
segments.s294-g120: که‌گر is the poetic contraction of که گر = “if.” The clause that follows is conditional: کوه پیش آمدی = “if a mountain came forward/advanced”; آمدی is a past/subjunctive-form verb meaning ‘came/should come.’ بر دو میل expresses distance/measure (“by/two miles”); میل here functions as a unit of distance (a metrical/older sense) rather than any of the listed lexical senses, so it is treated as a distinct, context-specific sense.
segments.s294-g17: از here is the preposition 'from/of' introducing a source or point of reference. آن (that) + نامور (renowned) form an epithet before the proper name خسرو (Khosrow). سرکشان is a plural adjectival/noun form attached to خسرو (appositive or epithet: 'the rebellious'); its lexical senses are not given in the supplied list, so its precise dictionary sense is marked unknown below.
segments.s294-g18: چنین بود is a predicative phrase 'thus/so was' with بود as the past copula. در پادشاهی places the predicate 'in kingship/kingdom' (در = in, پادشاهی = kingship/kingdom), and نشان functions as the noun 'sign/emblem' governed by the preceding clause: 'thus was (the) sign in the kingship.' Word order is typical Persian: predicator (چنین بود) followed by locative phrase and head noun.
segments.s294-g19: به introduces the goal/location 'to/at the frontier' (مرزی). که is a relative pronoun 'which/that' linking the frontier to the relative clause لشکر فرستاده بود. فرستاده here is the past participle 'sent' and بود is the auxiliary past 'had' (فرستاده بود = 'had sent'), so the clause reads 'to the frontier which he had sent an army to.'
segments.s294-g20: بسی is an adverbial quantifier 'much/many' (here treated as quantifying the following nouns). پند و اندرزها is a coordinated noun phrase 'advice and counsels' with اندرزها in plural (the -ها plural). داده بود is the compound verbal form 'had given' (داده = given, بود = past auxiliary), so the whole means 'he had given much advice and counsel.'
segments.s294-g21: چو/چون (both here) are simile markers 'like/as'. The first چو introduces لهراسب (Lohrasp) as an example; the second چون introduces اشکش (Ashkash). تیز چنگ is a noun phrase 'sharp (تیز) claws (چنگ)', where تیز is an adjective modifying چنگ. اشکش in this line appears as a name or epithet (not the 'tear' sense), but the supplied senses list does not include that reading (marked below as unknown).
segments.s294-g22: که is the relative pronoun 'which/that' introducing the clause that describes the claws. از + ژرف دریا = 'from the deep sea' (از = from, ژرف = deep, دریا = sea). ربودی is the past form 'seized' (third‑person singular past), so the clause reads 'which had seized a whale' (نهنگ = whale); the perfective past sense is used here.
segments.s294-g23: دگر means 'another' or 'other' and precedes the epithet نامور 'renowned' which modifies the proper name رستم (Rostam). پهلوان follows as a title/appositive 'champion/hero.' The sequence is a nominal apposition giving a new exemplary figure.
segments.s294-g24: This is a string of adjectival epithets describing Rustom: پسندیده here = 'chosen/approved' (sense 'chosen' rather than merely 'pleasing'), then و (and), راد = 'generous/noble', و (and), روشن‌روان = 'bright‑soul' or 'cheerful/clear‑souled'. The adjectives follow the noun in appositive style and enumerate his qualities.
segments.s294-g129: پس is a discourse adverb meaning “then/thereupon.” پشت ایشان is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning “behind them” (پشت + pronoun ایشان). سواران جنگ is a compound noun: سواران (plural “horsemen”) followed by جنگ which specifies their function/character (‘of battle’ → ‘battle-horsemen’). The sequence places the temporal adverb first, then the locative, then the noun phrase.
segments.s294-g130: برآگنده is a participial/adjectival form meaning “raised/upright/protruding.” ترک‌ش is written as the stem + clitic (ترک + ش) in the manuscript division here; the clitic ش is the possessive “his/its,” so together it functions as a possessed noun (here understood as quiver or a possessed item). ز is the poetic preposition “from/because of,” followed by تیر (arrow) and خدنگ (dagger) as a compound describing the type of arrow. The whole phrase describes the quivers/arrow-gear as set up/raised, with arrows like daggers.
segments.s294-g131: ز خاور is a prepositional phrase “from the east” (ز used poetically). سپاهی گزین کرد is the verbal clause: گزین (infinitive/stem ‘choose/select’) + کرد (past tense ‘he did’ → ‘he chose’). شاه appears at the end as the agent/topic (inverse word order common in epic Persian: verb before subject), so the line reads literally “from the east soldiers selected did the king.”
segments.s294-g132: سپردار is a noun ‘commander’ (here the person selected or appointed). با introduces an accompanying description ‘with’ (با درع و رومی کلاه = with armor and a Roman helmet). درع and رومی کلاه are coordinated modifiers: درع ‘armor’ and رومی کلاه ‘Roman (Byzantine) helmet’; note Persian freely places such modifiers after the noun they describe.
segments.s294-g133: ز گردان گردنکشان is a prepositional/partitive phrase ‘from the troop of گردنکشان.’ گردان is ‘troop/unit,’ گردنکشان (literally ‘neck‑breakers’) is a descriptive epithet (here ‘rebels’ or fierce men) modifying گردان; سی هزار follows as the numeral ‘thirty thousand’ that quantifies what is taken from that troop.
segments.s294-g134: فریبرز را is the definite object/topic marker construction: the name Фریبرز marked by را (direct object/emphatic topic). داد is the past tense ‘gave’ (he gave). جنگی سوار is a noun phrase in apposition/description: جنگی (‘warlike/military’) modifying سوار (‘horseman’/‘mounted warrior’), describing what sort of force was given.
segments.s294-g135: ابا appears here as a lexical item that can be read as a name or the verb/root meaning ‘to refuse’; in context it functions as a proper name introducing the person (Aba). شاه شهر دهستان تخوار is a chain of titles/locations in apposition: شاه (king) + شهر (city/town) + دهستان (district) + تخوار (place-name), with Persian allowing this stacking of nominative/attributive nouns.
segments.s294-g136: که introduces a relative clause ‘who/which.’ جنگ بداندیش is a compound where بداندیش (‘ill‑minded/malicious’) qualifies جنگ — together meaning ‘one with hostile/ill designs for war.’ بودیش is an archaic past copular form (‘it was’/‘he was’) agreeing with the relative, and خوار is a predicate adjective meaning ‘despised/humbled.’ The clause thus states his malicious war-intent and his resulting low/humbled status.
segments.s294-g9: A noun phrase: یکی here is an indefinite 'a/certain' introducing the item; تخت پیروزه is 'turquoise throne' (تخت = throne, پیروزه = turquoise/gem‑color). بر پشت پیل is a prepositional phrase 'on the back of the elephant' (بر = on; پشت = back; پیل = elephant). The whole segment supplies the object and its location before the verb.
segments.s294-g10: نهادند is the simple past plural 'they placed/put' (perfective). و شد links two clauses: 'and became' — شد is past 'became'. روی گیتی 'the face/surface of the world' is the subject of شد, and چو نیل is a simile 'like indigo' describing its appearance.
segments.s294-g11: نشست is past 'sat' (third person). The phrase از بر تخت is an older locative expression meaning 'upon the throne' (بر governs تخت; از here is part of the archaic locative formula). با تاج شاه 'with the king's crown' expresses accompaniment/instrument — 'with' + noun phrase (تاج = crown, شاه = king).
segments.s294-g12: خروش آمد is a nominative noun + verb construction: 'a cry/roar came' (آمد = came). از دشت وز بارگاه is a compound source phrase 'from the plain and from the palace'; وز is the poetic contraction of ו + ز (i.e. 'and from'), so the phrase lists two origins of the sound.
segments.s294-g13: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when/as'. بر پشت پیل is 'upon the elephant's back' (preposition بر + noun پشت پیل). آن شه نامور is the noun phrase 'that famed/renowned king' and functions as the subject of the following verbs.
segments.s294-g14: زدی is a past action verb 'he struck / he put / he performed' (classical narrative past). مهره در جام 'a bead/jewel into the cup' is the direct object and location (مهره = bead/jewel; در جام = in the cup). و بستی کمر joins the second past verb: بستی is 'he bound/fastened' (past), and کمر here means 'girdle/waist' (i.e. he girded his waist).
segments.s294-g15: نبودی is the negative past 'it was not' (or 'there was not'); here it negates the following infinitive construction. به هر پادشاهی 'for every kingship' (به = for/to; هر = every; پادشاهی = kingship/sovereignty) marks the domain to which the statement applies. روا means 'proper/allowed/fitting'.
segments.s294-g16: نشستن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to sit' and is the thing judged by روا; مگر is the exception marker 'except'. بر در پادشا is a classical locative 'at the king's door/threshold' (بر = on/at; در = door/threshold; پادشا = king), so the clause means sitting was not proper anywhere except at the sovereign's threshold.
segments.s294-g105: سپهدار (title) + گودرز (name) + را (object marker) precedes the verb; خواستند is 3rd-person plural past and here means “they summoned/asked for” rather than mere “wanted.” Word order is object (with را) before the verb, a common Persian pattern.
segments.s294-g106: چپ (= ‘left’) modifies لشکرش (‘his army/wing’ where -ش is the possessive suffix). را marks that phrase as the definite object; بیاراستند is a past 3pl verb (poetic/older form of ‘to set in order/array’) meaning “they arrayed/arranged.” The clause thus says they put the commander’s left flank in order.
segments.s294-g107: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning “he commanded/ordered.” تا introduces a purpose/subordinate clause (“so that / in order that”). پیش قلب سپاه is a prepositional phrase meaning “before/in front of the army’s heart” (i.e. the army’s center).
segments.s294-g108: به + پیلان جنگی is the instrument/means phrase “with/beside battle‑elephants” (پیلان is plural of پیل). ببستند is a 3rd‑person plural past form used in the subordinate clause after تا to express the intended action — here “they should/might close” or simply “they closed/blocked.” راه is the object ‘the road/way.’
segments.s294-g109: نهادند is past 3rd‑person plural of نهادن ‘to place/set’; here it means “they placed.” صندوق is the direct object “a chest.” بر پشت پیل is a prepositional phrase “upon the elephant’s back” (بر = on; پشت = back; پیل = elephant).
segments.s294-g110: زمین (the earth/ground) is the subject and شد is the copular/transformative past “became.” به کردار is a comparative phrase meaning “in the manner of / like,” and دریای نیل is a genitive phrase “the sea of the Nile” (i.e. like the Nile). So the clause reads literally “the ground became like the Nile’s sea.”
segments.s294-g111: هزار (a thousand) is the numeral subject (not listed among the provided words here). از marks the partitive/genitive “of”; دلیران is the plural noun “the brave ones/warriors”; روز نبرد is a genitive/temporal phrase “the day of battle” specifying which group. Together: “a thousand of the brave of the battle‑day.”
segments.s294-g112: به صندوق marks the target “at/against the chest.” بر before ناوک gives the sense “against/with (a) spear” and ناوک is “spear/arrow.” انداز + کرد functions as a light‑verb compound: انداز (root meaning related to ‘throw/launch’) together with کرد (‘did/made’) yields “hurled/threw.” Grammatically, انداز behaves like a verb stem used with کرد as auxiliary. The clause therefore says they hurled spears at the chest.
segments.s294-g33: دگر here means 'the other' or 'another' and precedes پهلوان to form 'the other champion.' طوس is in apposition (the proper name Tus) attached to that noun. زرینه کفش is a compound epithet: زرینه (golden) modifies کفش (shoe), placed after the name as a postpositive descriptive phrase (typical in poetic Persian), so the whole segment reads 'the other champion, Tus, (the) golden‑shod.'
segments.s294-g34: کجا is the interrogative adverb 'where.' بود is the past tense copula 'was' (the verb of existence) and takes an unexpressed subject (referring back to طوس). با + کاویانی درفش is a prepositional phrase 'with the Kayanian standard' where کاویانی is an adjectival/attributive form modifying درفش (banner). The order is adverb + verb + accompaniment; the subject is supplied from the previous segment.
segments.s294-g35: به is the preposition 'to/for.' هر is the distributive 'every/each' modifying نامداری (a notable/person of renown) and, joined by و, خودکامه‌ای means 'and a self‑willed one' (خودکامه with the indefinite/agentive -ای). The whole phrase means 'to every notable and to any self‑willed person.'
segments.s294-g36: نبشتند is the past plural 'they wrote/inscribed.' بر + پهلوی here indicates the linguistic/mediumal sense 'in Pahlavi' (i.e. in the Pahlavi tongue/script). نامه‌ای is the indefinite direct object 'a letter.' The word order is verb + prepositional phrase marking language + object; 'they wrote a letter in Pahlavi to every notable...' flows across the two segments.
segments.s294-g37: فرستادگان is the plural subject 'the envoys.' خواست is the past 'asked/wished/desired.' از انجمن is the prepositional object 'from the assembly' (از = 'from/of', انجمن = 'assembly/meeting'). The clause means 'the envoys asked from the assembly.'
segments.s294-g38: This is a coordinated description of the person requested: زبان‌آور is 'eloquent' (literally 'tongue‑bearing' used as 'eloquent/spokesman'), بخرد is 'wise' (بخرد here is an adjective meaning 'sage/prudent'), and رای زن is a compound meaning 'one who gives counsel' (رای = 'counsel/plan' + زن functioning as an agentive/verbalizer here). Note: زن in رای زن is not 'woman' but an agentive element (so none of the simple provided senses exactly match that use).
segments.s294-g39: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'that/who.' پیروز is an epithet 'victorious' placed before the name کیخسرو (Kay Khosrow). از پشت پیل is a prepositional phrase 'from behind the elephant' (از = 'from', پشت = 'behind/rear', پیل = 'elephant'). The phrase identifies the agent and the spatial point of action.
segments.s294-g40: بزد is the past tense 'he struck' (transitive). مهره is the direct object here meaning 'game piece/figurine/piece' (a tactical piece). و is the coordinating 'and.' گشت is the intransitive 'became/turned' (change of state) with گیتی 'the world' as its subject, and چو + نیل is a simile 'like the Nile' (چو = 'as/like', نیل = 'the Nile'), so 'and the world became like the Nile.'
segments.s294-g65: یکی آنک is an archaic indefinite + relative construction: یکی = 'a certain/one' and آنک = relative 'who/that'. بر + کشوری marks the domain ('over a country/realm'), and شاه بود is the predication with بود as the past copula ('was king'). Note that آنک functions like a relative pronoun tying the opening 'a certain one' to the rest of the clause.
segments.s294-g66: گه here is an adverb meaning 'sometimes' or 'at times'. The clause RHEME is 'رزم ... همراه بود' where همراه (literally 'together/companion') functions as a predicate adjective meaning 'accompanied' or 'was with' and بود is the past copula. Word order places the battlefield noun رزم before the predicate 'was accompanied by fortune'.
segments.s294-g67: دگر = 'another/other' introduces a second example; it modifies شاه کرمان 'the king of Kerman'. The relative particle که introduces the subordinate temporal phrase هنگام جنگ = 'at the time of war' (هنگام + noun).
segments.s294-g68: نکردی is negative past ('ن' + کرد + final -ی, an archaic/poetic form of the past) meaning 'did not do'. به دل is an adverbial phrase 'in the heart' (به + دل), یاد رای = 'memory/recall of counsel' (یاد = remembrance; رای = counsel/plan), and درنگ = 'pause/delay'. Together the clause says he did not, inwardly, delay to recall or ponder counsel — i.e. he did not hesitate.
segments.s294-g69: چو introduces a comparison or enumeration ('as/like' or 'when'); here it begins a new example: 'as for ...'. صیاع فرزانه is an epithet phrase (صیاع modifying/being linked to فرزانه 'wise/sage'), followed by شاه یمن 'king of Yemen' (noun + genitive place). In this poetic register epithets and names are packed together before the copula is implied.
segments.s294-g70: دگر again marks 'another' (another example/person). شیر دل is a compound epithet 'lion-hearted' (شیر 'lion' + دل 'heart'), placed before the proper name ایرج. پیل تن is another compound epithet 'elephant-bodied' (پیل 'elephant' + تن 'body'). The copula ('was') is omitted as is normal in such enumerative lines; the epithets directly qualify the name.
segments.s294-g71: که is the relative 'who/that'. بر شهر کابل = 'over the city of Kabul' (بر marking jurisdiction/over). The item بد او is an archaic/dialectal form (here acting like an older dative/prepositional phrase 'to/for him' or a linking element) followed by پادشا 'king'. In context the clause means 'who was king over the city of Kabul'; بد is an archaic particle not represented by the provided senses.
segments.s294-g72: A string of epithets joined by و 'and': جهاندار (literally 'world-holder', i.e. sovereign) + بیدار ('awake', here 'alert, vigilant') + فرمان روا (a compound meaning 'one who rules by command' — فرمان 'command' + روا used adjectivally). Together they enumerate qualities of his rule; word order is a simple coordinate list.
segments.s294-g89: چو at the start is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The following sequence lists names/groups in the nominative position: گرگین is the proper name Gorgin, میلاد here is used as a proper name (not the common noun 'birth'), and گردان ری reads as 'the troop (گردان) of Rey (ری)' with ری as a genitive/attributive place-name.
segments.s294-g90: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they departed/they went'. یکسر follows as an adverb meaning 'all at once' or 'together'. به فرمان is the prepositional phrase 'at the command (of...)' and کی here functions as the question/relative 'who' (understood as 'the king' in context, but grammatically 'who').
segments.s294-g91: پس is a sentence-linking adverb 'then'. پشت او را is the object phrase 'his rear / his back' with را marking it as definite object. نگه داشتند is a compound verb: نگه (watch/keeping) + داشتند (past tense 'they had/kept'), together meaning 'they kept watch' or 'they guarded'.
segments.s294-g92: همه is 'all/everyone'. نیزه is 'spear' (direct object). از here means 'from' or 'from on high', and ابر literally 'cloud' — poetically 'from above'. بگذاشتند is past plural 'they placed/they let down' (here 'lowered' fits the context of spears).
segments.s294-g93: به رستم سپرد is 'he handed/entrusted to Rostam' (به + proper name + سپرد 'entrusted'). آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time'. میمنه here means 'the right (side/flank)' — a technical/poetic military term.
segments.s294-g94: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. بود is the past copula 'was'. او is the third‑person pronoun 'he'. سپاهی شکن is a compound nominal: سپاهی 'warrior/army' + شکن 'breaker', i.e. 'army‑breaker' or 'breaker of armies'. یک‌تنه is an adverb/adjective meaning 'single‑handedly' or 'alone', qualifying his prowess.
segments.s294-g95: هر آنکس که is a generalizing relative 'whichever person who / whoever'. از زابلستان marks origin 'from Zabulistan'. بدند is an archaic/formal past‑plural or modal form meaning 'they were' (used here in the generalizing clause 'whoever was...').
segments.s294-g96: وگر is a conditional 'and if / even if'. کهتر is an adjective 'smaller/lesser/inferior'. و is the simple coordinating 'and'. خویش + the enclitic ش (ش = his) yields 'his own/related to him' with خوی signifying kinship. دستان is plural of 'دست' here likely 'men of the hand / followers' (literally 'hands' used for retainers/people), and بدند repeats the past/plural copular/auxiliary 'they were'. Grammatically this is a concessive clause ('even if they were lesser and his own men').
segments.s294-g49: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. شد ساخته is a compound verbal expression: شد (past auxiliary/‘became’/‘was’) plus ساخته (the past participle 'made/assembled'), so together 'was assembled' or 'was made ready'. جنگ را contains the object-marker را after جنگ ('the war'), marking the purpose/object for which the army is assembled; لشکری is the noun 'an army' describing what was assembled. Word order is poetic but functions as 'When an army was assembled for the war...'.
segments.s294-g50: ز introduces origin/source 'from', followed by هر نامداری 'every famous/noted one' — here indicating the army’s men were drawn from famous people. به هر کشوری is a locative phrase 'in/from every country/land' (به + noun phrase marking the realm). The whole phrase qualifies the composition of the army: 'from every notable in every country'.
segments.s294-g51: از آن پس is an adverbial phrase 'after that / from then on'. بگردید is the past/preterite (3rd plural) of گردیدن 'to turn / to move about' (here 'they turned' or 'the host turned'), and گرد plus the following noun سپاه functions adverbially as 'around the host' or simply indicates the host as the thing that turned — poetic inversion places گرد before سپاه.
segments.s294-g52: بیاراست is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'well‑arrayed / adorned', describing the army. بر هر سوی رزمگاه is a prepositional directional phrase: بر ('toward / on') + هر سوی ('each side/direction') + رزمگاه ('battlefield'), indicating that the army is arrayed toward every side of the battlefield.
segments.s294-g53: گزین کرد is a two‑word past tense verb: گزین 'choose' plus کرد (past auxiliary 'did/made') giving 'he chose/selected'. زان لشکر نامدار (زان = 'from that') is the object phrase 'from that renowned army/host' — زان shows selection taken out of that body.
segments.s294-g54: سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen / riders'. شمشیر زن is a compound where شمشیر 'sword' combines with زن (here verbal, 'striker') to mean 'sword‑wielding' or 'swordsmen' — a compound adjective/noun describing the riders. (The numeral 'سی هزار' that follows gives their number: thirty thousand.)
segments.s294-g55: که begins a relative clause 'who / that'. باشند is the 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/present form of بودن 'to be' used here in a relative sense ('who would be / who were'), indicating accompaniment. با او 'with him' is the comitative phrase, and به قلب اندرون is a locative phrase 'in the very heart / inner core' (به + قلب + اندرون), specifying closeness/intimacy of support.
segments.s294-g56: همه جنگ را is 'the whole war' (همه modifying جنگ, with را as the object/definiteness marker). دست شسته به خون is a nominal phrase 'hands washed in blood' where دست 'hand' is literal and شسته is the past participle 'washed'; به خون ('in/with blood') marks the instrument/substance — together an idiom meaning 'bloodstained / having washed their hands in blood' describing their violent participation in the fighting.
segments.s294-g161: This is a nominal clause: 'شه غرچگان بود' = 'he was king of the roarers'. Word order is Subject (شه) + modifier (غرچگان) + copula (بود). The phrase 'بر سان شیر' follows as a simile: بر + سان + noun = 'in the manner of / like a …'. Here بود is the past copula 'was' linking the subject to its predicate.
segments.s294-g162: This is a short interrogative/exclamatory clause. 'کجا' functions as a rhetorical 'where/how' introducing the question. 'ژنده' is an adjective before 'پیل' (elephant) meaning 'ragged/tattered'; 'آوریدی' is the 2nd-person past 'you brought'. The prepositional phrase 'به زیر' = 'to/under/beneath' completes the action: 'you brought (it) under (someone/your control)'.
segments.s294-g163: 'به دست منوچهرشان' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into the hand/control of their Manuchehr' (به ... دست = into the hand/power). The verb 'جای کرد' (literally 'made place') is a causative/putting verb meaning 'placed' or 'appointed'. Word order: prepositional phrase → verb.
segments.s294-g164: Here 'سر تخمه را' is the definite object ('سر' = 'chief/head' of the 'تخمه' = 'lineage/offspring') marked by the object particle 'را'. 'لشکر آرای کرد' uses آرای (from آراییدن/آرایش) with کرد to mean 'arrayed/marshaled the army' or 'set the army in order'. Verb 'کرد' completes the causative action.
segments.s294-g165: 'بزرگان' is the subject 'the great ones'. The relative/connector 'که' introduces the subordinate clause 'از کوه قاف آمدند' = 'who came from Mount Qaf'. Word order is typical Persian: subject → relative clause with verb at the end ('آمدند').
segments.s294-g166: This clause describes manner/instruments and the manner of arrival. 'ابا' here functions as an archaic/poetic form of 'با' (= 'with') introducing the instruments 'نیزه و تیغ' ('spears and swords'). 'لاف آمدند' is a verbal phrase meaning 'they came boasting' (لاف = boast; آمدند = they came). The verb 'آمدند' is the clause-final past plural.
segments.s294-g167: 'سپاهی' is the noun 'an army/host'. The preposition 'ز' (poetic variant of 'از') introduces origin: 'ز تخم فریدون و جم' = 'from the seed/offspring of Fereydun and Jam'. Word order is noun → origin phrase.
segments.s294-g168: 'پر از خون دل' is an adjectival phrase meaning 'full of the blood of the heart' (figuratively 'bloodied / grieving hearts'); 'پر' = 'full (of)'. The following 'از تخمه زادشم' repeats origin/lineage 'from the offspring/stock of Zadshem' (زادشم is a proper name). The clause as a whole qualifies the army: 'an army ... full of heart's blood, from the stock of Zadshem.'
segments.s294-g201: بهر سو is a prepositional phrase meaning “toward that side” with بهر = “toward/to” and سو = “side”; the noun طلایه (“vanguard”) is the object/topic. پدیدار کرد is a past-tense, transitive verbal phrase literally “made (it) appear” (پدیدار + کرد), where کرد functions as the past auxiliary/causative and the subject (he/they) is omitted as is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s294-g202: سر خفته is a noun + participle construction: سر = “(the) chief/head (here ‘chief’)” and خفته = postpositive adjectival participle ‘sleeping’. از خواب بیدار کرد is a causal/periphrastic expression: از = “from”, خواب = “sleep”, بیدار = “awake”, کرد = past ‘made/caused’, so literally “made (him) awake from sleep” = “roused/awakened.” Subject remains omitted and is the same actor as the previous clause.
segments.s294-g203: به هر سو is the locative/directional phrase “to every side.” برفتند is the simple past, 3rd-person plural “they went/advanced.” The noun کارآگهان (plural) follows as the (postposed) subject/agent — an epic word meaning ‘those who are informed/aware’ i.e. scouts/observers — a common poetic word order where the verb precedes the subject.
segments.s294-g204: همی is an old/narrative adverb often marking ongoing or immediate action in epic Persian; جست is the past of ‘to leap/spring’ so “they leapt/sprang.” بیدار is an adjective ‘awake/alert’ modifying the agent, and کار جهان = “the affairs/business of the world.” The clause therefore reads as an absolute/actional statement: “alert, they sprang to the world’s affairs.”
segments.s294-g205: کجا here is an interrogative/rhetorical ‘why/where’ opening a reproach; کوه is the locus ‘on the mountain’ and بد دیده‌بان is a noun phrase ‘a poor/ill sentinel’ with بد (postpositive/attributive) qualifying دیده‌بان. داشتی is the 2nd-person singular past ‘you had/you placed’ (subject تو omitted). The whole phrase asks/rhetorically reproaches why a bad watch was kept on the mountain.
segments.s294-g206: سپه را uses the object marker را to mark the definite object ‘the army.’ پراگنده is a predicative adjective ‘scattered.’ نگذاشتی is the negative past 2nd-person form of گذاشتن used here in the sense ‘you did not allow/prevent/left (it) so’ — together meaning ‘you did not prevent the army from being scattered’ or more naturally ‘you failed to keep the army from scattering.’
segments.s294-g207: همه begins an enumerative list ‘all’ followed by a syndetic list of places (کوه و غار و بیابان و دشت) — standard noun + و + noun coordination for ‘mountains and caves and deserts and plains.’ The list functions as a scene-setting noun phrase.
segments.s294-g208: به هر سو = ‘to every side.’ همی is the epic narrative adverb (continuous/instant action marker). گرد here has the sense ‘around/about’ (indicating movement or encircling) and لشکر is the subject ‘the army/host.’ بگشت is a past tense verbal kernel meaning ‘turned/returned/rounded about’ (archaic/poetic form of گشتن used in narrative). The clause says that the host turned about in all directions.
segments.s294-g153: دگر لشکری کز خراسان بدند = 'another army who/were from Khorasan.' دگر (fronted) means 'another/other' and modifies لشکری ('troops, an army'); the relative clause begins with کز (archaic/poetic relative ‘that/who…’), followed by the place خراسان. بدند is the old poetic plural copula/preterite 'they were' used here to predicate 'were from Khorasan.' Word order places the modifier دگر before لشکری and the relative clause after it.
segments.s294-g154: جهانجوی و مردم شناسان بدند = 'they were world‑seekers and knowers of men.' جهانجوی is an agent noun 'world-seeker/ambitious person' (here plural in sense), و links the two predicates, مردم شناسان is a compound meaning 'knowers/understanders of people' (شناسان is an agent noun formed from شناختن), and the repeated بدند is the plural past/copula 'they were,' tying these predicates back to the army mentioned before.
segments.s294-g155: منوچهر آرش نگهدارشان = 'Manuchehr, Arash — their guardians.' Two proper names are placed in apposition to the role expressed by نگهدارشان (نگهدار + شان 'their'), a noun meaning 'guardian/keeper.' The sequence names the protectors; no copula is repeated here because the predicate is carried over from poetic syntax.
segments.s294-g156: گه نام جستن سپهدارشان = 'at times (they) sought the name/title spahbed for them.' گه is an adverb 'sometimes/occasionally,' نام جستن is an infinitival noun phrase 'seeking a name' (i.e. seeking to call or bestow a title), and سپهدارشان is 'their spahbed/commander' (سپهدار + شان). The implied subject is the same as the previous clause; the construction describes an occasional act (jending a title).
segments.s294-g157: دگر نامداری گروخان نژاد = 'another famed/notable, Gurukhan by lineage.' دگر 'another' modifies نامداری ('renown/notability' used as a substantive, 'a famed one'). گروخان نژاد places the name/title گروخان together with نژاد ('lineage/stock'), i.e. 'Gurukhan of that lineage' or 'Gurukhan by birth.' The phrase presents a new noteworthy person by name and pedigree.
segments.s294-g158: جهاندار وز تخمه کیقباد = 'a world‑ruler (جهاندار) from/of the seed/offspring of Kay Qobad.' جهاندار is an epithet 'sovereign/conqueror'; وز here is an archaic fused particle (written وز in the manuscript) functioning like 'و ز' or 'از' to mean 'from/of' (this sense is not among the provided dictionary options); تخمه means 'seed/offspring' and کیقباد names the ancestral king. The phrase gives his royal descent.
segments.s294-g159: کجا نام آن شاه پیروز بود = 'what a famous/victorious king he was.' کجا here functions as an exclamatory/poetic particle ('how/what'), naming the extent of fame (نام) of آن شاه ('that king'); پیروز 'victorious' qualifies شاه. بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause is an exclamatory assertion of the king's renown.
segments.s294-g160: سپهبد دل و لشکر افروز بود = 'he was a spahbed who delighted heart and army.' سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (military commander); دل و لشکر افروز is a compound epithet: دل‌افروز 'heart‑gladdening' and لشکر‌افروز 'army‑gladdening/illuminating' joined by و. بود is the past copula linking the title and the epithets to the king described before.
segments.s294-g41: This is a pair of short optative wishes. 'مه آرام' is an old poetic adjective phrase meaning 'great calm' (مه = 'great/mickle', آرام = 'calm'). 'بادا' is the optative particle 'may it be'. 'شما را' marks the beneficiary 'to you' (شما = you; را = object/recipient marker). The second noun 'مه خواب' repeats the pattern: 'a great sleep' wished for the same addressee; the two noun phrases are linked as coordinated wishes governed by the single optative 'بادا'.
segments.s294-g42: 'مگر' here introduces an exception and means 'unless'. 'ساختن' is the verbal infinitive/participial idea 'to make/prepare' used here as an active process ('is preparing'). 'رزم' is the object 'war/battle', and 'افراسیاب' is the agent placed after the verb phrase (a poetic word order): 'unless Afrasiab is making/raising a battle.'
segments.s294-g43: چو = 'when'. آن نامه = 'that letter'. برخواند is the past tense (third-person) of برخواندن 'to read aloud/recite'; in this poetic order the verb precedes its subject, which follows as 'هر مهتری' ('each notable/chief') — so the clause reads 'when each notable read that letter aloud.'
segments.s294-g44: This is a dependent interrogative clause introduced by 'کجا' = 'where'. 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. 'در پادشاهی' = 'in the kingdom/realm'. 'سری' here means 'secret/hidden' (the thing kept secret) — so the clause reads 'where in the kingdom was the secret.'
segments.s294-g45: 'ز' (poetic for از) = 'from'. 'گردان گیتی' is a genitive phrase 'the hosts/array of the world' (گردان = troop/host, گیتی = world). 'برآمد' is past 'arose/issued forth'. 'خروش' is the noun 'cry/uproar' — together: 'from the world's hosts arose a cry.'
segments.s294-g46: 'زمین' = 'the earth'. 'همچو دریا' = 'like the sea' (simile introduced by همچو). 'برآمد' again 'arose'. 'به جوش' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in a boil/surge' or 'seething'; here it describes how the earth 'rose' — i.e. 'the earth, like the sea, rose up in a surge.'
segments.s294-g47: 'بزرگان هر کشوری' = 'the great ones (notables) of every country' (بزرگان = great ones; هر کشوری = of each realm). 'با سپاه' = 'with an army' or 'with their forces' — a simple prepositional accompaniment marking that they come accompanied by troops.
segments.s294-g48: 'نهادند' is the past third-person plural of نهادن 'to place/set' — here a transitive verb 'they set/put'. 'سر' (lit. 'head') is the direct object, used idiomatically for 'themselves' or 'their heads' (i.e., they directed themselves). 'سوی درگاه شاه' = 'toward the royal court' (سوی = toward, درگاه = royal threshold/court, شاه = the king). The whole means they moved/turned their heads (i.e. presented themselves) toward the king's court.
segments.s294-g73: هر آنکس که is a correlative relative phrase meaning 'whoever' or 'anyone who'. از تخمه کیقباد is a prepositional/genitive phrase: از ('from/of') + تخمه ('seed, offspring') + کیقباد (proper name), i.e. 'of the seed/line of Kay Qobad'. The relative clause is left open and links to the following appositional description.
segments.s294-g74: بزرگان بادانش و بانژاد is an appositional noun phrase describing that person: بزرگان (plural 'great ones' or 'notables') followed by two comitative/qualifying compounds بادانش (با + دانش, 'with knowledge' = 'learned') and بانژاد (با + نژاد, 'by lineage' = 'of noble birth'). The adjectives/compounds follow the head noun in this poetic ordering.
segments.s294-g75: چو is the subordinating particle 'when/as'. شماخ is likely a proper name (here treated as a name/title). سوری شه سوریان strings epithets: سوری (as an ethnonym/epithet 'of Suri'/'Syrian') + شه (poetic form of 'شاه', 'king') + سوریان ('the Syrians'); together they identify the named ruler as 'Shomakh the Suri, king of the Syrians'.
segments.s294-g76: کجا functions as 'where'. رزم را is the definite object ('the battle' + object marker را). بود here is the past copula 'was' and بسته is a past participle/adjective 'tied/closed/arranged' used idiomatically for 'the battle was joined/engaged'; میان 'between' completes the phrase. The verb+participle order (بود بسته) is archaic/poetic: 'was (so) that the battle was joined between (them)'.
segments.s294-g77: فروتر از او is a comparative phrase 'lower than him' (فروتر = 'lower/more inferior' + از + pronoun او). گیوه رزم زن is a compound noun phrase: گیوه ('traditional shoe' — here descriptive, 'sandal‑clad') modifying رزم‌زن ('one who strikes in battle' = 'fighter'); note زن here is the agentive sense derived from زدن ('to strike'), giving 'fighter'. The whole phrase describes someone of lower rank but a fighting man.
segments.s294-g78: به هر کار پیروز is a prepositional phrase 'victorious in every deed' (به = 'in/with respect to', هر کار = 'every action', پیروز = 'victorious'). لشکر شکن is a nominative apposition 'breaker of armies' (لشکر 'army' + شکن 'breaker'), reinforcing the fighter's prowess. Adjectives follow the noun in this poetic listing.
segments.s294-g79: که introduces a relative clause 'who'. بر شهر داور is a prepositional phrase meaning 'judge over the city' (بر = 'over/on', شهر = 'city', داور = 'judge/arbiter'). بد او پادشا is a poetically compressed clause; بد here is an archaic/poetic form functioning as a copular/past auxiliary equivalent to بود ('was') and links to او پادشا ('his king'). In short: 'who was judge over the city; his king was...'. (The word بد does not match the negative senses listed and is being used here as an archaic copula.)
segments.s294-g80: جهانگیر و فرزانه و پارسا lists the king's epithets: جهانگیر is used as a title/name ('Jahangir' or 'world‑conqueror'), followed by coordinating adjectives فرزانه ('wise') and پارسا ('pious'). The series is a simple noun/adjective list describing the king.
segments.s294-g25: بفرمودشان is a past‑tense, honorific/periphrastic verb meaning “(he) commanded/ordered them”; the clitic -شان marks the indirect object “them.” بازگشتن is the infinitive “to return/send back.” به + در literally “to the door/gate,” here meaning the royal gate/court (destination), with به marking direction and در = gate/place.
segments.s294-g26: هر آن کس که introduces a free relative ‘whoever/any person who’: هر = every/each, آن کس = that person/one, and که is the relative pronoun. بد and پرخاشخر are predicative adjectives describing that person; گرد here is uncertain/archaic in form and is part of the descriptive phrase (see note). و simply joins the two adjectives.
segments.s294-g27: در گنج بگشاد is an action clause: در گنج = in/at the treasury (در = locative), and بگشاد is a past form ‘opened’ (b‑ + گشاد). و links the following clause. روزی بداد is a short verb phrase where روزی (provision/sustenance) is the object and بداد (ب + داد) functions as past‑tense ‘gave’ (archaic compound form).
segments.s294-g28: بسی از روان پدر کرد یاد is a verbless/nominal cluster turned into an action by کرد: بسی = much/abundantly (adverbial), از روان پدر = ‘of/from the soul of (his) father’ with از marking the genitive/relationship, کرد یاد = lit. ‘made remembrance’ = ‘remembered/commemorated.’ Here کرد is the light verb that makes یاد (remembrance) into a verbal action.
segments.s294-g29: سه تن = ‘three persons’ (سه numeral + تن = persons). را is the definite/object marker attached to the noun phrase. گزین کرد is the compound past ‘chose/selected’: گزین (choose) + کرد (did) and زان انجمن = ‘from that assembly’ (زان = ز + آن = from that; انجمن = assembly). Word order is SOV in the clause: object (سه تن را) before verb (گزین کرد).
segments.s294-g30: This segment lists the three chosen types in apposition: سخن‌گوی is a compound meaning ‘speaker’ (سخن = speech, گوی = one who utters). روشن‌دل is a compound adjective/noun ‘bright‑minded’ or ‘clear‑hearted.’ تیغ‌زن is a compound ‘sword‑striker,’ i.e. swordsman. The coordinating و connects the three nouns.
segments.s294-g31: چو at the clause head is the simile/temporal conjunction ‘as/like.’ رستم is the name. که introduces a relative clause ‘who…’. بد here is an archaic/poetic element whose exact force in context is uncertain (it can be negative in modern Persian but sometimes serves emphatically in older usage); it modifies پهلوان (champion). پهلوان بزرگ = ‘great champion.’ Overall the phrase reads ‘as Rostam, who (truly) was a great champion.’
segments.s294-g32: Another simile introduced by چو: چو گودرز ‘as Goudarz.’ بینادل is a rare/uncertain epithet (manuscript/lexical readings vary); its precise sense is uncertain here and it functions as an epithet modifying Goudarz. آن پیر گرگ = ‘that old wolf’ (آن = that/the, پیر = old/elder, گرگ = wolf), a figurative epithet meaning an experienced, fierce veteran. Word order is standard: epithet(s) after the name.
segments.s294-g209: عنانها is the plural object 'the reins' (the -ها plural is explicit). یک اندر دگر is a conventional classical Persian adverbial phrase meaning 'one within another / one to the other' and follows the object; ساخته is a perfect/participle form 'made / bound' (here past action: 'having bound'). The sequence is object + adverbial phrase + participle so the clause reads 'he bound the reins one within another.'
segments.s294-g210: همه جنگ را is a noun phrase with the object marker را: 'the whole host / all the fighting-force' (همه = 'all/everyone'; جنگ used collectively for the fighting/host). گردن افراخته is a postposed adjective phrase literally 'neck(s) raised' (گردن 'neck'; افراخته past participle 'raised/erect'), functioning as the predicate describing the host as holding their necks high (i.e. proud/ready).
segments.s294-g211: This is an impersonal/existential negative construction: از ایشان کسی را نبد بیم و رنج = 'from them to anyone there was not fear and suffering.' از ایشان marks source/causation ('from them'); کسی را ('to anyone', with را as an indirect object/topic marker) is the affected person; نبد is an archaic negative past form meaning 'was not' (negating existence/occurrence); بیم و رنج = 'fear and suffering.'
segments.s294-g212: همی راند is a habitual/imperfective past meaning 'he continually drove / carried / took (along)'; همی marks continuity/habit. با خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'with himself' (indicating accompaniment). شاه گنج is a juxtaposed noun phrase functioning as the thing taken along: 'the king's treasure' (in poetry the genitive is often by simple juxtaposition without ezāfe). Thus: 'he constantly took the king's treasure with him.'
segments.s294-g213: بر این‌گونه چون is a temporal/conditional phrase 'when/if in this way' or 'thus when'; بر here is a prepositional particle 'upon/in respect to.' شاه is the subject 'the king'; لشکر is the direct object 'an army/host' and بساخت is a past tense verb 'assembled / made up' (classical past of بسازیدن), so the clause means 'when the king thus assembled an army.'
segments.s294-g214: به گردون is an adverbial phrase 'toward the sky/heaven' (به + گردون). کلاه کیی is a compound nominative/accusative 'the Kayanian cap/hat' where کیی is the Kayanian epithet; poetic Persian commonly juxtaposes possessor and possessed without ezāfe. برفراخت is the past verb 'raised/erected' (بر + افراخت), so the clause reads 'he raised the Kayanian cap to the sky.'
segments.s294-g215: دل مرد is a genitive/attributive cluster 'the heart of a man' (classical juxtaposition without ezāfe). بدساز is an adjective modifying مرد meaning 'ill‑made / ill‑disposed / ill‑tempered' and follows the noun it qualifies; با نیک خوی is a prepositional phrase 'with good disposition/temper' modifying the whole phrase. The result is a descriptive noun phrase: 'the heart of an ill‑disposed man, with good nature...' (poetic tension between بدساز and با نیک خوی).
segments.s294-g216: جز از جنگ introduces an exception: 'except/from war'; جستن is the infinitive 'to seek' (classical infinitive form), نکرد is the past negative 'did not do / had not' used here with آرزوی 'wish' to mean 'had no wish except to seek war' (literally 'except for seeking war, he did not do [other] wishing'). The clause therefore means 'he desired nothing except to seek war.'
segments.s294-g97: بدیشان is an archaic contracted form of به ایشان (to them) and functions as the indirect object. سپرد is a simple past transitive verb 'handed/entrusted'. آن زمان is an adverbial time phrase 'at that time' placed after the verb (permissible in poetic word order). دست راست is the direct object ('right hand' = right wing/position); note the object marker را is omitted (common in poetry).
segments.s294-g98: همی before a past tense verb gives an ongoing or emphatic past sense (classical narrative imperfective). نام و آرایشِ جنگ is a coordinated compound direct object: نام (fame/reputation) and آرایش (arrangement/array) governed by جنگ (of battle). خواست is the simple past 'he wanted/sought' with its subject understood from the prior clause.
segments.s294-g99: سپاهی is the noun 'soldiers/army' (direct object). گزین کرد is a compound verbal expression in the past ('chose/selected'), where گزین is the lexical verb and کرد is the past-tense auxiliary. بر + میسره is a prepositional phrase indicating placement or direction ('on/upon the meysara'); in this line the phrase modifies where the chosen troops were placed. Word order (object – verb – adverbial) reflects poetic flexibility.
segments.s294-g100: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. خورشید تابان is a noun phrase 'the shining sun' (چو + NP = 'like the shining sun'). ز برج is a prepositional phrase 'from/from the tower'. بره here is a figurative noun 'lamb' used in the image; syntactically it completes the simile phrase. The whole simile modifies the manner in which the troops are set or appear.
segments.s294-g101: سپهدار گودرز is a nominal phrase with title + name 'the commander Goudarz' as subject. کشواد is a nominative complement (another name or epithet) and بود is the past copula 'was'. The structure is a simple equational clause 'X was Y' with the copula in past tense.
segments.s294-g102: This segment lists additional proper names joined by و. هجیر is a name; و is the coordinator 'and'. چو here functions idiomatically as a linker introducing the next name (in poetry چو can mean 'and/also' or 'as'), followed by شیدوش (a name/epithet) and another و + فرهاد. بود is the past copula applying to the whole coordinated list (they were).
segments.s294-g103: بزرگان is the head noun 'the great ones / nobles'. که is a relative pronoun 'who/which' introducing a subordinate clause that gives their origin. از + بردع و اردبیل is a prepositional phrase 'from Bardʿ and Ardabil' (از marks origin or source). Grammatically the clause specifies which great men are meant.
segments.s294-g104: به پیش جهاندار is a locative phrase 'in the presence of the sovereign' (به پیش = before/in front of, جهاندار = sovereign). بودند is the past plural copula 'they were'. خیل is the predicate noun 'a host / a crowd' placed after the verb in poetic word order; together the clause says that these men constituted a host before the king.
segments.s294-g193: 'بدان' is an imperative: 'know this' (classical hortative). 'تا' introduces purpose ('so that' / 'in order that'), and 'ببندد' is the present/subjunctive third‑person singular (prefix بـ + بندد) meaning 'he may bind/close/restrain'. 'ز' here is the poetic preposition 'from', and 'بیداد دست' is a genitive-like compound 'the hand of injustice' (دست = 'hand/power' governed by بیداد = 'oppression').
segments.s294-g194: 'کسی را' uses کس with the object/topic marker را; here it functions as 'anyone' (object of restraint). 'کجا نیست یزدان‌پرست' is a verbless relative/qualifying phrase: کجا نیست = 'who is not' / 'who is nowhere' (here read as 'who is not'), followed by یزدان‌پرست 'God‑worshipper / pious person'. Together they qualify 'کسی' as 'anyone who is not God‑fearing.'
segments.s294-g195: نباشد is a negative jussive/subjunctive ('may there not be' / 'let there not be'); here it governs an existential wish: 'نباشد کس' = 'let there be no one'. 'از خوردنی بی‌نوا' uses از + noun to indicate deprivation ('devoid of/without food') and بی‌نوا is an adjective/noun meaning 'poor, destitute'. Grammatically: 'نباشد کس از خوردنی بی‌نوا' = 'may no one be poor of food' i.e. 'let no one be without food.'
segments.s294-g196: This clause is idiomatic: 'روا ندارد' = 'is not permissible'. ستم 'oppression' is the subject, نیز 'also/likewise' links this prohibition to the previous clause, and 'بر کس' ('against anyone') uses بر with کس. The literal order 'ستم نیز بر کس ندارد روا' = 'oppression likewise against anyone has not permissibility' → 'oppression is likewise not permissible against anyone.'
segments.s294-g197: The phrase 'جهان پر ز گردون بد' uses پر ز + genitive to mean 'the world is full, by/through the sky (گردون), of evil (بد)' — گردون here implies fate/heaven. The conjunction و links گاومیش ('water‑buffalo') into the image; the line places a concrete creature side by side with the abstract 'badness' for a moral observation.
segments.s294-g198: 'ز بهر' = 'for the sake of' (ز + بهر), 'خورش' is used as 'food/dish' (object), and را is the definite object marker attached to خورش. همی before راند gives an iterative/continuous sense ('keeps / continually'), and راند is past/stative form 'drives' used here descriptively. پیش = 'forward/ahead' completes the motion image: 'drives forward for its food.'
segments.s294-g199: 'بخواهد' (from خواستن) is third‑person singular ('he wishes' / 'let him wish'); in classical Persian initial verbs can set the clause: 'بخواهد همی هرچ باید ز شاه' reads as 'Whatever the king wishes (بخواهد), whatever must be (هرچ باید) from the king (ز شاه) ...' — effectively: 'Let whatever the king wants be done.' همی is an emphatic/continuous particle here.
segments.s294-g200: 'به هر کار' = 'in/for every affair', 'باشد' is a jussive/subjunctive 'may it be', and 'زبان سپاه' literally 'the tongue/speech of the army' (زبان = 'speech/voice' here). The clause means 'may the army's voice be present in every matter' — i.e. the army's (or force's) opinion/command should hold in all affairs.
segments.s294-g145: This is a nominal phrase in apposition: two nouns linked by و ('and') are followed by the proper name زهیر, which identifies them. سر مایه here functions idiomatically as 'chief/principal' (not anatomical 'head'), and پیشروشان contains the third-person plural possessive suffix -شان ('their'), so the phrase reads 'their chief and their vanguard, Zuhair.'
segments.s294-g146: که introduces a relative clause modifying زهیر: 'who ...'. ربودی is an archaic/simple past (3rd sing.) 'took/snatched'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and چنگال شیر is a genitive construction 'the claw of the lion' (literally 'claw [of] lion'). The whole clause: 'who seized a gazelle from the lion's claw.'
segments.s294-g147: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb 'he commanded/said'. تا here marks purpose/result ('so that'/'until'), introducing the following clause. نزد + نستوه شد literally means 'became near/in the presence of the distressed one' — صدرıly 'came to the distressed one's presence' (نزد used as 'in the presence of'), with شد as the past/copular verb 'became/arrived'.
segments.s294-g148: چپ لشکر شاه is a genitive phrase 'the left (wing) of the king's army' (چپ = 'left' functioning as 'left flank'). چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and کوه شد is 'became a mountain' (شد as copula/verb of becoming) — a poetic hyperbole describing solidity or mass.
segments.s294-g149: This is a noun phrase: سپاهی 'army/host' followed by بد modifying it as 'ill/unfavorable' (here 'hostile' or 'formidable'), and از روم و بربرستان gives the origin 'from Rum and Barbaristan.' Word order is typical Persian: noun + modifier + prepositional origin.
segments.s294-g150: گوی here functions as an introductory reportive ('they say' / 'it is said') — an elliptical, poetic present used to introduce a name. The rest is an equational phrase with the copula omitted: 'پیشرو نام لشکرستان' = 'the vanguard's name (was) Lashkarestan.' Persian often omits 'to be' in such reported-name constructions.
segments.s294-g151: سوار و پیاده lists two categories 'horsemen and footmen' (cavalry and infantry). بدی precedes the numeral and here qualifies the force as hostile/ill (an intensifying epithet); سی هزار is the numeral 'thirty thousand' modifying the whole preceding phrase (i.e., thirty thousand mounted and foot soldiers). Note Persian allows numeral after the qualified noun phrase.
segments.s294-g152: برفتند is past plural 'they went/advanced.' با is the preposition 'with,' ساقه here is a poetic word for 'shaft' or 'staff' (often used for a standard or pole), and شهریار is the genitive 'of the king.' Together: 'they advanced with the king's shaft/standard' (i.e., under the king's banner or in the king's company).
segments.s294-g169: از این دست here = 'of/from this sort'. The verb-less noun phrase puts the preposition از before the demonstrative + دست; دست (literally 'hand') is used figuratively as 'kind, class'. The numeral سی هزار follows the noun phrase (a common poetic order).
segments.s294-g170: This continues the noun phrase: جهاندار 'world‑ruler/sovereign' qualifies those thirty thousand. وز here is an archaic contraction (sense not listed) equivalent to وِ ز (and/of/from), linking to تخمه شهریار 'offspring of the monarch' — تخمه used as 'offspring/lineage' and شهریار 'king'.
segments.s294-g171: سپرد is past 'entrusted/handed over'. Classical word order places the verb before its noun phrases: the following این سپه is the thing handed over and گیو is the agent (subject); گودرز را is marked with را as the definite recipient/object. Thus: 'Giv handed over this army to Goudarz.'
segments.s294-g172: بدو = 'to him' (the prepositional pronoun); تازه شد is a past intransitive 'became fresh/renewed'. دل همه مرز را is the affected phrase (دل 'hearts' + همه مرز 'of the whole frontier') with را marking definiteness/emphasis — literally 'to him became fresh the hearts of the whole frontier' → 'to him the hearts of the whole frontier were renewed.'
segments.s294-g173: به یاری = 'to/for help' ('in aid'); the second به + پشت means 'to the rear/behind' and سپهدار گیو = 'commander Giv' (title + name). The whole prepositional phrase sets the place/role: they moved to Giv's support at his rear.
segments.s294-g174: برفتند is past plural 'they went/advanced'. The following گردان is the troop (subject) described by adjectives بیدار 'alert' and نیو 'new' (an adjectival/poetic usage). Word order places the verb before its subject, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s294-g175: فرستاد is past 'he sent/dispatched'. بر میمنه = 'on the right (flank)' (بر + میمنه indicating direction/location). ده هزار gives the number 'ten thousand' (the quantity phrase follows the location here in poetic order).
segments.s294-g176: This noun phrase names the force: دلاور سواران 'brave horsemen' (دلاور modifies سواران). خنجر گزار is a compound meaning 'dagger‑bearers/‑wielders' (گزار here functions as an agentive/participial element in the poem; the provided senses do not capture this use).
segments.s294-g185: به پیش اندرون = a prepositional phrase 'in front of / before the inner chamber' (به + پیش + اندرون). قارن رزم زن is a name Qaren followed by an epithet 'رزم‌زن' (one who strikes in battle) used appositionally; the verb ('stood' / 'was') is omitted and understood. Word order: location phrase first, then name + epithet.
segments.s294-g186: سر نامداران آن انجمن = a genitive construction 'the head/chief of the renowned ones of that assembly' (سر = 'head, chief' + نامدارانِ آن انجمن with the ezāfe omitted). سر is the title/role; نامداران is a plural substantive 'the renowned (men)'.
segments.s294-g187: بدان تا = 'know that' (بدان is an imperative/stative 'be aware/know'; تا introduces the content clause 'that'). میان دو رویه سپاه = 'between the two faces/fronts of the army' (میان = 'between'; دو رویه = 'two sides/surfaces/fronts'; سپاه is the possessor 'of the army' with the ezāfe omitted).
segments.s294-g188: بود گرد اسب افگن و رزمخواه = 'there was/there stood around gleaming horses and war-seeking fighters.' بود is the past copula 'was' (existential); گرد = 'around/about' (giving the sense 'all around'); اسب افگن is a noun phrase where افگن modifies اسب ('gleaming/bright horses'); رزمخواه is an agentive compound 'those who desire battle' = eager fighters.
segments.s294-g189: از آن پس = 'after that' (temporal phrase). به گستهم گژدهم گفت = 'he said to Gostaham and Gozhdaham' (به introduces the indirect objects, here two personal names; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said').
segments.s294-g190: که با قارن رزم زن باش جفت = indirect command 'that (you) be paired with Qaren the warrior.' که introduces the reported clause; با + name = 'with Qaren'; رزم‌زن is the epithet 'warrior' (رزم + زن, here زن is agentive 'one who strikes'); باش is the imperative/subjunctive 'be'; جفت is predicate 'a pair / paired (mate)'.
segments.s294-g191: بفرمود = an honorific / classical past verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces the purpose/command clause. اندمان پور طوس = 'the company/assembly of the son of Tūs' (اندمان = company/assembly; پور = 'son of' used in a name/genitive; طوس = Tūs, here the person). The ezāfe linking is omitted in the poetic style.
segments.s294-g192: بگردد = subjunctive/optative present 'should go around / roam' (ب‑ prefix + گردد). به هر جای = 'to every place' (به = to; هر = every; جای = place). با پیل و کوس = 'with elephants and drums' (با = with; پیل = elephant; کوس = war-drum). The subjunctive is controlled by تا/بفرمود (he ordered that ...).
segments.s294-g81: به (preposition) + دست چپ خویش is an instrumental/locative phrase meaning “with / at his left hand/side.” Here به is used in the sense “by/with” and comes before the noun phrase دست چپ خویش (دست = “hand,” چپ = “left,” خویش + ش = the reflexive possessive “his/own”), then بر پای کرد is a verb phrase where بر (on/upon) + پای (foot/side) with کرد (simple past “did/made/placed”) expresses placing someone at that position. In short: “he placed (someone) at his left side.”
segments.s294-g82: دلفروز را is the definite/direct object marked by را (object marker). لشکر آرای here is a noun phrase meaning literally “army‑adorner/army‑ornament” (آرای in the sense of adornment), and کرد (past of کردن) is the main verb “made/appointed/caused.” The object دلفروز را is linked to this verb: “he made Delfruz (into) the army’s adornment.”
segments.s294-g83: بزرگان (pl. “the great ones / nobles”) is the subject. که introduces a relative clause (“who/that”). از تخم = “from the seed/of the stock of,” followed by پورست: here an attributive name/epithet attached to تخم (i.e. “of Pour’s stock” or “Pour‑born”). تیغ (sword) at the end is the object that will be acted on in the following verb phrase. Word order places the relative/background phrase immediately after بزرگان before the verb.
segments.s294-g84: زدندی is the past plural verb “they struck/drew/struck with” (archaic/poetic past ending -ندی for they). شب تیره is an adverbial or circumstantial phrase “the dark/gloomy night.” بر باد میغ literally places میغ (blade) with بر باد (“to/onto the wind”), producing the image “the blades were sent off/whirled on the wind.” The clause is vivid, with poetic word order (object and adverbial elements arranged for meter and imagery).
segments.s294-g85: هر آنکس که بود او = “whoever was he / every one who was” (هر آنکس = “whoever / each person who”); بود is past-tense “was.” ز تخم = poetic variant of از تخم “from the seed/of the stock,” followed by زرسب as the name/epithet of that stock (an adjective or tribal/name label). The clause establishes origin/lineage (“whoever came from the seed of Zarsab”).
segments.s294-g86: پرستنده is a noun meaning “worshipper / devotee / follower” (agent noun formed from پرستیدن). The following words form a theonym/epithet: فرخ آذر گشسب — taken together as a name or honorific (فرخ “fortunate/happy,” آذر “Azar” which can be read as name/‘fire’, and گشسب the heroic name Gushasp). Grammatically this phrase appositive/attributive: “a devotee of Farukh‑Azar Gushasp.”
segments.s294-g87: دگر at the start functions as an additive/adversative adverb here (“besides/other/again”), then a triad of proper names follows: بیژن (Bijan), گیو (Giv) و رهام (and Roham). گرد after the names is an adverbial/participle-like word (poetic usage) meaning roughly “around / about” or signalling the group/assembly; together the phrase sets up these heroes as the other named persons being spoken of.
segments.s294-g88: کجا is an interrogative adverb “where / in what place/way.” شاهشان is a possessive noun “their king” (شاه + شان). از بزرگان is a prepositional phrase “among/from the great ones,” and شمرد is the past tense of شمردن “to count / reckon / consider.” Together this forms a rhetorical question: “Where would their king count them among the great ones?” (i.e. in what rank/place would the king reckon them?).
segments.s294-g121: دل (subject) + adjective سنگ placed after it: a common Persian pattern 'دل سنگ بگذاشتندی' means 'they made/turned their hearts to stone' (hardened themselves). بگذاشتندی is a past/perfective 3rd-person plural (archaic literary form of گذاشتند) meaning 'they put/placed/made'. به تیر is a prepositional phrase 'toward/against the arrow' marking the cause/target of that hardening.
segments.s294-g122: This is an inverted, compressed clause: نبودی کس = 'there was no one' (نبودی is past of 'to be' used here negated); آن زخم را is the definite object 'that wound' (را marks the object/emphatic topic), and دستگیر here is adjectival/nominal meaning 'one to succor/help' (i.e. 'a helper/succorer'). Together: 'there was no one to succor that wound.'
segments.s294-g123: پیاده functions as noun 'foot soldiers/infantry'. پس here means 'after/behind' (indicating position). پیل کرده is a perfective participial phrase ('having followed the elephant' or 'who had come after the elephant') where کرده is the past participle used to form a descriptive clause modifying پیاده. به پای is a prepositional phrase 'to/at the (elephant's) feet' indicating their proximity.
segments.s294-g124: ابا ... نه is an archaic expression: ابا is the root meaning 'reluctance/to refuse', here negated by نه to mean 'without reluctance' (i.e. unhesitating). رشی is an adjectival descriptor 'bearded' modifying the person; نیزه is 'spear'; سرگرای is an adjectival/participial form meaning 'occupied/engaged with' (here 'busied himself with the spear' or 'wielding the spear'). Word order places descriptive adjectives and participles after the head noun, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s294-g125: سپرهای is the plural noun 'shields' (the -های plural). گیلی is an adjective following the noun (classical order) meaning 'clayey' or 'made of clay' (describing the shields or their poor quality). به پیش اندرون is a locative/adverbial cluster: به پیش 'in front' and اندرون 'within/close to the body' — together describing the shields being held forward close to the chest.
segments.s294-g126: همی is the classical continuous/intensifying particle often placed before verbs (here marking the vivid/perceptual action). از is the preposition 'from/out of'. جگرشان is a possessive noun 'their jigar' — poetically 'their heart/seat of feeling' (jigar = heart/courage here). بجوشید is past 'boiled/gushed/seethed' and خون is the thing that boiled (blood); word order places the verb before the noun for poetic emphasis: 'blood boiled from their hearts.'
segments.s294-g127: پیاده again 'foot soldiers'. صفی in classical Persian context means 'a rank/row/formation' (here 'a rank of foot soldiers'); the supplied senses did not include this meaning, so the appropriate sense index is unavailable. از پس is a compound locative 'from/after/behind' indicating they follow, and نیزه‌دار is a compound noun 'spear‑bearer/spearman' used as the reference point these foot soldiers follow.
segments.s294-g128: سپردار is 'commander' or literally 'one who has an army/host' (here 'commanders' or armed men). با is the preposition 'with' introducing accompaniment or instrument. تیر is 'arrow' (here likely 'with arrows' i.e. archers), and جوشن‌گذار is a compound noun 'one who bears the mail/armor' (mail‑bearer/armourer); the cluster lists the accompanying armed men.
segments.s294-g57: به یک دست is an instrumental phrase meaning 'with one hand' or 'by a single gesture' (به here used in instrumental sense). مر is the old-object pronoun 'me' placed before the object (proclitic), طوس را is the definite object (را marks Tus as object), and کرد جای is the verb + noun sequence 'made (into) a place/position' = 'set (someone) in place (of someone else)'. Word order is: instrument — (object pronoun) — object — object marker — verb — noun showing the result/ne position.
segments.s294-g58: This is an appositive/epithet describing the person placed: منوشان is a proper name (here used as a name), خوزان qualifies it as 'of Khuz/Khuzan', and فرخنده رای is a compound adjective phrase 'of fortunate/auspicious counsel' (فرخنده modifies رای). The whole segment labels Tus with this epithet.
segments.s294-g59: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/for that' (classical connective). بر کشور خوزیان is a locative phrase 'over the country of the Khuzis' (بر = 'over/on' governing کشور), بود is the past-tense copula 'was/reigned', and شاه is the subject/predicate 'king'.
segments.s294-g60: بسی here is an adverbial/intensifying word meaning 'many/much' (classical usage 'very/many'), naming the quantity of following men; نامداران is a plural noun 'renowned ones', زرین qualifies them as 'golden', and کلاه is the noun 'hats/caps' — an adjectival postnominal structure common in epic Persian (adjective before or after noun).
segments.s294-g61: دو تن is the subject 'two persons' (تن = person), نیز is the adverb 'also', بودند is the past plural copula 'were', and هم‌رزم is the predicate noun 'comrades‑in‑arms'. سوز is given appositionally (a proper name); here it names one of those comrades.
segments.s294-g62: چو functions as a comparative/relational particle 'as/like' (classical use), introducing a likeness or example. گوران شه is a titular/name phrase 'Gūrān‑shah' (Gūrān + shah/title). آن is the demonstrative 'that', گرد here is a noun that in context is part of a phrase with لشگر (either 'circle/host' or a proper-name element), and لشگر فروز is a noun + adjective 'the shining/bright army' (فروز postmodifies لشگر). The segment links the comrades to that bright host or to a named leader of that host.
segments.s294-g63: وز او is an archaic/clitic sequence meaning roughly 'and of him' or 'from him/among them' (وز here functions like وَ + از in older usage). نیوتر is an epithet/qualifier attached to آرش (likely a byname), and آرش is the personal name. رزم زن is a compound noun formed by a noun + verbal element: رزم 'battle' + زن (from زدن 'to strike') producing 'one who strikes in battle' = 'warrior' (here زن is deverbal and supplies the agentive sense).
segments.s294-g64: به هر کار is a prepositional phrase 'in/at every affair/deed' (به used adverbially with هر کار), پیروز is a predicative adjective 'victorious', و coordinates, and لشکرشکن is a compound adjective/noun 'army‑breaker' (one who routs or destroys armies). Together they describe Arash's martial prowess.
segments.s294-g177: This is a nominal phrase headed by سپه (army/host). ده هزار is a numeral quantifier specifying the size; از + دلیران literally 'of the brave ones' marks the source/constituent group (literally 'an army from the brave men'). گرد here functions as a collective/assembled notion (a participial/collective element used in older Persian) completing the noun phrase: 'an army composed of ten thousand brave men.'
segments.s294-g178: پس is an adverb ('then/afterwards'). پشت is a locative/relational noun 'rear/behind' and marks where the following proper names are placed. گودرز and کشواد are proper names in apposition (here two people placed together), and برد is the past tense of بردن 'to take/bring/put' (classical narrative past). The whole segment means that (they) put/took Goudarz and Keshvad to the rear.
segments.s294-g179: دمادم is an adverb meaning 'again and again' or 'constantly.' بشد is the past/preterite form of شدن used impersonally here ('appeared/it came to pass that'). برته تیغ‌زن functions as a name or epithet: تیغ‌زن 'sword-wielder/swordsman' is modified by یا appositive برته (here treated as a personal/epithet), so the clause means 'Barteh, the swordsman, appeared repeatedly.'
segments.s294-g180: This is the locative continuation: ابا کوهیار is a compound name/epithet (Aba‑Kuhyar) placed before اندر آن انجمن ('in that assembly'). اندر is a classical preposition 'in/within'; آن is the demonstrative 'that'; انجمن is 'assembly/meeting.' Grammatically, the phrase attaches to the previous clause to say where Aba‑Kuhyar (the swordsman) appeared.
segments.s294-g181: به مردی is an indirect/beneficiary phrase 'to/for a man' (به + indefinite مردی). شود is the present/past-meaning of شدن used here as 'came to be' or 'became.' جنگ را uses the definite object/topic marker را to mark 'war' as the domain; یار 'companion/ally' is the predicate complement and گیو is the proper name; together: 'Giv became (the) companion in war (to a man).' The clause has a somewhat compressed classical word order where the beneficiary and domain precede the copular verb.
segments.s294-g182: This is an appositive description of Giv: سپاهی 'a warrior/warriorly one' modified by سرافراز 'honored/triumphant.' و is the coordinating 'and.' گردان here means 'troop/command' and نیو (classical form of نو) means 'new/fresh.' Together this gives an epithet-like description: 'a proud/triumphant warrior and leader of a fresh troop.'
segments.s294-g183: زواره is a proper name (here read as a place or person launching forces). بد in classical Persian can mean 'ill' or 'bad' and functions as an epithet qualifying زواره. این جنگ را uses را to mark 'this war' as the domain, and پیشرو 'vanguard/forerunner' names the element that Zowareh provides. The phrase thus says that Zowareh (ill‑favored) furnished the vanguard for this battle.
segments.s294-g184: This is a descriptive nominal group: سپاهی 'soldiers/army,' همه 'all/every,' جنگ سازان literally 'war-makers' (here 'fighters/warriors'), and نو 'new/fresh.' The sequence describes the vanguard as 'an army composed entirely of new fighters.' Note the adjective-like words follow the nouns in typical classical Persian order.
